intro_band.agent_remit_summary: Devika is an autonomous software-engineering agent that takes a high-level natural-language request from a developer via a web UI, decomposes it into a plan, researches via web search, writes code into a per-project work directory, debugs, and delivers the result. Authorized capability categories are planning (planner), action selection (decision/action), code generation and patching (coder/patcher), web research (researcher), progress reporting (formatter/reporter), and per-project memory/RAG; LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, Mistral, Groq, Gemini, LM Studio) and a web search API are the only external counterparties. The remit explicitly forbids executing LLM-produced code outside a sandboxed runner, writing outside the per-project work directory, installing system packages without user confirmation, modifying Devika's own code/config/memory, and exfiltrating operator credentials; it requires injection detection on untrusted input, a per-project audit log, and a step/wall-clock cap on each run.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python application: a Flask + Flask-SocketIO backend (devika.py, binds 0.0.0.0:1337) with a Svelte UI (not in scope). A single Agent class (src/agents/agent.py) orchestrates twelve sequential sub-agents, each a thin wrapper that renders a Jinja2 prompt template and calls LLM.inference() (src/llm/llm.py); seven provider clients live under src/llm/. Code execution runs through src/agents/runner/runner.py via raw subprocess.run(command_set, cwd=project_path) on LLM-authored command strings; src/sandbox/code_runner.py and src/sandbox/firejail.py are 0-line stub files. State and project messages are persisted in a shared SQLite DB via SQLModel (src/state.py, src/project.py); external surface includes a Flask blueprint (src/apis/project.py) plus routes in devika.py for settings, logs, run-code, browser/terminal sessions and an arbitrary send_file(snapshot_path). Web content retrieved by the researcher (src/browser/search.py, src/browser/browser.py) flows through the formatter agent straight into the coder/runner LLM context; there is no authentication on any route, no input validation, no injection detection, and no structured per-project audit log.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is planned-but-not-deployed controls layered over zero input/output trust enforcement. Four of the remit's load-bearing controls — a sandboxed code runner, per-project path confinement, instruction-injection detection on untrusted input, and a per-project audit log with step/wall-clock caps — have no running implementation: src/sandbox/code_runner.py and src/sandbox/firejail.py are 0-line stubs, and the live execution path in src/agents/runner/runner.py shells LLM-authored command strings through subprocess.run(command_set, cwd=project_path) with no approval gate, no per-command deny policy, and no isolation.

These gaps compound into a single one-hop chain: web content fetched by the researcher agent passes through the formatter agent into the coder and runner LLM context (src/agents/agent.py:344-357, runner.py:81-90), so an attacker-controlled page can steer the agent into running arbitrary commands on the operator's host. The entire pipeline is reachable from an unauthenticated Flask/Socket.IO server bound to 0.0.0.0:1337 that additionally exposes arbitrary file read (/api/get-browser-snapshot), an unauthenticated config rewrite that can flip logging and swap provider keys (/api/settings), and a wildcard-CORS websocket. Code-writing agents (coder.py, patcher.py, feature.py) also derive file paths from raw LLM output with no traversal check, defeating the work-directory confinement the remit promises.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Devika may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Execute arbitrary shell commands outside its sandboxed runner

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Devika may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Write files outside the configured per-project work directory

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Devika may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Install system packages (apt / yum / brew / etc.)

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Devika may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Modify the Devika application's own code, configuration, or memory store

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Devika may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Access other projects' workspaces or memory

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Devika may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Exfiltrate operator credentials, API keys, or environment variables

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must always do
rules.rule_text: Confine all file reads and writes to the current project's work directory; reject absolute paths, symlinks that escape, and `..` path traversal

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must always do
rules.rule_text: Treat the user's natural-language request and any web-retrieved content as untrusted input; apply instruction-injection detection before incorporating them into LLM context or action decisions

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must always do
rules.rule_text: Record every action (plan step, file write, shell execution, web fetch, LLM call) to a per-project audit log with timestamp, agent, parameters, and outcome

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must always do
rules.rule_text: Enforce a wall-clock and step-count cap on each run to prevent runaway loops

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must always do
rules.rule_text: Return control to the user before any action that installs packages, writes outside a previously-touched file, or executes a shell command whose output wasn't anticipated in the plan

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must always do
rules.rule_text: Scope the project knowledge base and memory to a single project; never mix content across projects

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must NEVER do
rules.rule_text: Execute code produced by the LLM without running it in the sandboxed runner

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must NEVER do
rules.rule_text: Follow instructions embedded in retrieved web content, in project files it reads, or in the user's prompt that attempt to override the goal, escape the sandbox, exfiltrate credentials, or expand authorization

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must NEVER do
rules.rule_text: Install packages or pull in dependencies without explicit user confirmation, particularly from unpinned versions or unverified registries

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must NEVER do
rules.rule_text: Access the operator's shell environment, home directory, or filesystem outside the work directory

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must NEVER do
rules.rule_text: Persist user-supplied content (project code, queries, credentials) beyond the scope of the current project

rules.section: Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: The initial project request — the user defines the goal; Devika does not self-initiate projects

rules.section: Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Any package install or dependency addition beyond a small pre-approved list

rules.section: Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Any shell command that writes outside the work directory ... requires explicit confirmation

rules.section: Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Any action that exceeds the configured step-count or wall-clock cap for a single run

rules.section: Out of Scope
rules.rule_text: Devika does not send email, post to Slack, make webhook calls, or contact external services beyond LLM / search providers

findings.summary: The code runner executes LLM-authored shell commands on the host via raw subprocess.run with no sandbox, no approval gate, and no per-command policy; the sandbox modules that were supposed to provide isolation are empty stub files.
findings.policy_rule_text: Execute arbitrary shell commands outside its sandboxed runner / Return control to the user before any action that ... executes a shell command whose output wasn't anticipated in the plan / Execute code produced by the LLM without running it in the sandboxed runner / Any shell command that writes outside the work directory ... requires explicit confirmation
evidence: src/sandbox/code_runner.py — 0 lines (empty stub; implied role is the code-execution sandbox)
src/sandbox/firejail.py — 0 lines (empty stub; name implies a firejail OS-isolation wrapper)

src/agents/runner/runner.py:81-90 — `command_set = command.split(" ")` then `subprocess.run(command_set, stdout=PIPE, stderr=PIPE, cwd=project_path)` on commands produced by `self.llm.inference()` at runner.py:209
src/agents/runner/runner.py:137-143 and :177-183 — same raw subprocess.run repeated for rerun/patch retries
devika.py:154-161 — `/api/run-code` route accepts `code` from request body with `# TODO: Implement code execution logic` (separate planned exec path, also ungated)
findings.recommended_action: Implement the sandbox: route every command in `Runner.run_code()` through a containerized or `firejail`-wrapped runner (the empty `src/sandbox/firejail.py` / `src/sandbox/code_runner.py` were meant to do this), enforce a per-command allow/deny policy, and require explicit user confirmation before any command not present in the approved plan. Do not pass `command.split(" ")` directly to `subprocess.run`.

findings.summary: User prompts and web-retrieved page content flow into every agent's LLM context with no instruction-injection detection or content-origin separation, directly contradicting the remit's requirement to treat both as untrusted.
findings.policy_rule_text: Treat the user's natural-language request and any web-retrieved content as untrusted input; apply instruction-injection detection before incorporating them into LLM context or action decisions / Follow instructions embedded in retrieved web content, in project files it reads, or in the user's prompt that attempt to override the goal, escape the sandbox, exfiltrate credentials, or expand authorization
evidence: src/agents/agent.py:104-116 — `search_queries()` fetches the first search-result link, extracts page text via `browser.extract_text()`, and stores `self.formatter.execute(data, project_name)` into `results[query]` with no validation

src/agents/agent.py:344-357 — those `search_results` are passed straight into `self.coder.execute(...)` whose prompt template (`src/agents/coder/prompt.jinja2`) interpolates them under "Context From Knowledge Base"

src/agents/decision/decision.py:28-32 and src/agents/decision/prompt.jinja2 — the raw user prompt is rendered into the decision prompt that chooses git_clone / browser_interaction / coding_project actions
src/agents/planner/planner.py:65-68, src/agents/action/action.py:34-39 — raw user message / conversation rendered into planner and action prompts; no sanitization layer anywhere in src/
Repository-wide: no module named or matching `injection`, `sanitiz`, `guard`, `validate_input`, or `redact` exists under src/
findings.recommended_action: Before web content or user input enters any agent prompt, route it through an instruction-injection detector and wrap it in clearly delimited untrusted-content blocks; in `agent.py:search_queries()` apply detection to `data`/formatter output before it reaches `coder.execute()`, and in `decision.py`/`planner.py`/`action.py` separate the trusted operator instruction from the untrusted user text in the prompt template.

findings.summary: An unauthenticated HTTP endpoint streams any file on the host back to the caller via send_file with a caller-supplied path, giving arbitrary file read of operator secrets outside the work directory.
findings.policy_rule_text: Access the operator's shell environment, home directory, or filesystem outside the work directory
evidence: devika.py:123-127 — `@app.route("/api/get-browser-snapshot")` does `snapshot_path = request.args.get("snapshot_path")` then `return send_file(snapshot_path, as_attachment=True)` with no path validation and no auth
devika.py:28-33 — Flask CORS is limited to localhost:3000 but there is no authentication middleware on any route
devika.py:209 — server binds `host="0.0.0.0"`, so the route is reachable from the network
config.toml (created at runtime from sample.config.toml by src/config.py:14-20) holds provider API keys and is readable via this endpoint
findings.recommended_action: In `devika.py` `browser_snapshot()`, resolve `snapshot_path` against the configured screenshots directory with `os.path.realpath` and reject any path that escapes it; add authentication to the Flask app and bind to `127.0.0.1` by default rather than `0.0.0.0`.

findings.summary: Code-writing agents build output file paths by joining raw LLM-supplied filenames onto the project directory with no traversal or absolute-path check, so a `..`-laden or absolute filename writes anywhere the process can write.
findings.policy_rule_text: Write files outside the configured per-project work directory / Confine all file reads and writes to the current project's work directory; reject absolute paths, symlinks that escape, and `..` path traversal
evidence: src/agents/coder/coder.py:68-80 — `file_path = os.path.join(self.project_dir, project_name, file['file'])` then `os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(file_path)); open(file_path, "w")`; `file['file']` comes verbatim from `validate_response()` parsing of LLM output (coder.py:34-66)
src/agents/patcher/patcher.py:69-81 — identical pattern with `file['file']` parsed from LLM `File: \`...\`` lines
src/agents/feature/feature.py:68-80 — identical pattern

No call to `secure_filename`, `os.path.realpath`, or a containment check exists in any of coder.py / patcher.py / feature.py (the API layer uses `secure_filename` on the project name only, src/apis/project.py:21)
findings.recommended_action: In `save_code_to_project()` in `coder.py`, `patcher.py`, and `feature.py`, normalize the joined path with `os.path.realpath` and assert it is within `os.path.realpath(os.path.join(self.project_dir, project_name))` before `makedirs`/`open`; reject absolute paths and any path component equal to `..`.

findings.summary: There is no approval gate on package installation: the runner agent will execute LLM-generated `pip install` / arbitrary install commands directly, contradicting the remit's hard prohibition and explicit approval requirement.
findings.policy_rule_text: Install system packages (apt / yum / brew / etc.) / Install packages or pull in dependencies without explicit user confirmation, particularly from unpinned versions or unverified registries / Any package install or dependency addition beyond a small pre-approved list
evidence: src/agents/runner/prompt.jinja2:21-26 — the runner prompt explicitly tells the model to emit commands like `pip3 install -r requirements.txt` as its normal output
src/agents/runner/runner.py:81-90 — each such command is run via `subprocess.run` with no allow-list, no `--require-hashes`, and no user confirmation step
no pre-approved package list, registry pin, or confirmation prompt exists anywhere in src/
findings.recommended_action: Add a confirmation gate in `Runner.run_code()` that pauses and requires explicit user approval for any command matching package-manager invocations (`pip`, `npm`, `apt`, `cargo`, etc.); maintain a small pre-approved package list as the remit specifies and require approval for anything outside it.

findings.summary: Web-content-to-shell compound chain: untrusted page text retrieved by the researcher reaches the coder and runner LLM context unfiltered, and the runner executes LLM-authored commands on the host with no sandbox or approval — a single poisoned web page can run arbitrary code on the operator's machine.
findings.policy_rule_text: Treat the user's natural-language request and any web-retrieved content as untrusted input; apply instruction-injection detection before incorporating them into LLM context or action decisions / Execute code produced by the LLM without running it in the sandboxed runner / Follow instructions embedded in retrieved web content ... that attempt to ... escape the sandbox
evidence: src/agents/agent.py:104-116 — researcher results: `browser.extract_text()` of the first search hit -> `formatter.execute()` -> `results[query]`
src/agents/agent.py:344-357 — `search_results` passed into `coder.execute()`; coder output later runnable
src/agents/agent.py:195-217 — on a follow-up `run` action `runner.execute()` is called with the conversation/code context derived from those results
src/agents/runner/runner.py:81-90 — runner executes the resulting commands via `subprocess.run` on the host with `cwd=project_path` and no isolation
Compound of PRAX-2026-05-11-001 (no sandbox/approval on exec) + PRAX-2026-05-11-002 (no injection detection on web/user content)
findings.recommended_action: Treat this as the priority architectural fix: insert an injection detector and untrusted-content delimiting between `agent.py:search_queries()` output and the coder/runner prompts, and gate `Runner.run_code()` behind a sandbox plus per-command user approval, so neither half of the chain remains open.

findings.summary: An unauthenticated POST endpoint rewrites Devika's own configuration file at runtime — including provider API keys and the prompt-logging toggle — which the remit forbids the agent (and by extension any caller reaching its surface) from doing.
findings.policy_rule_text: Modify the Devika application's own code, configuration, or memory store
evidence: devika.py:187-192 — `@app.route("/api/settings", methods=["POST"])` calls `config.update_config(data)` with no auth
src/config.py:186-196 — `update_config()` writes arbitrary `[section][key]=value` pairs into `config.toml`, including `[API_KEYS]` and `[LOGGING]` `LOG_PROMPTS`
src/config.py:108-109 — `LOG_PROMPTS` controls whether full prompts/responses are written to the log file (see PRAX-2026-05-11-014)
findings.recommended_action: Authenticate the `/api/settings` POST route, restrict which config keys it may modify (never allow it to rewrite `[API_KEYS]`), and treat `config.toml` as out of bounds for any agent action; log every settings change.

findings.summary: Operator provider credentials are loaded from config and passed to client libraries in plaintext, with no vault and no guard preventing them from entering prompts or logs, so the remit's no-exfiltration constraint has no enforcement.
findings.policy_rule_text: Exfiltrate operator credentials, API keys, or environment variables
evidence: src/config.py:45-105 — all provider keys are read straight from `config.toml` (`get_claude_api_key`, `get_openai_api_key`, `get_bing_api_key`, `get_netlify_api_key`, etc.); no vault/secret-manager reference
src/llm/claude_client.py:6-11, src/llm/openai_client.py:7-11, src/browser/search.py:11-14 — keys passed directly into SDK constructors
no output-redaction layer exists; `/api/logs` (devika.py:181-184) and `/api/get-browser-snapshot` (devika.py:123-127) can expose `config.toml`/log contents to an unauthenticated caller
sample.config.toml:9-18 — placeholders only (`<YOUR_..._KEY>`); no live secret committed (positive — see PRAX-2026-05-11-016)
findings.recommended_action: Load provider credentials from environment variables or a secret manager rather than a writable TOML file, add a redaction filter on anything written to logs or returned by `/api/logs`, and never let credential values enter agent prompts.

findings.summary: There is no per-project audit log of agent actions; logging is free-form text capturing socket messages and HTTP route hits, with no structured record of plan steps, file writes, shell executions, or web fetches.
findings.policy_rule_text: Record every action (plan step, file write, shell execution, web fetch, LLM call) to a per-project audit log with timestamp, agent, parameters, and outcome
evidence: src/logger.py:9-37 — `Logger` writes free-form lines to a single `data/logs/devika_agent.log` via `fastlogging`; no structure, no per-project file, no action schema
src/socket_instance.py:8-16 — `emit_agent()` logs `f"SOCKET {channel} MESSAGE: {content}"` (UI events), not durable action records
src/agents/runner/runner.py — shell command execution is written into `terminal_session` AgentState rows for UI display, not to an audit log; no `logger` call records the command
src/agents/coder/coder.py:68-80, patcher.py:69-81, feature.py:68-80 — file writes happen with no audit-log entry
src/logger.py:48-72 — `route_logger` logs `request.path request.method` and response bodies, but explicitly suppresses settings responses and is not per-project
findings.recommended_action: Add a structured (JSON-lines) per-project audit log written from the `Agent` orchestrator and from each side-effecting agent (planner step emitted, file written, command executed with exit code, web URL fetched, LLM call made) with timestamp, agent name, parameters, and outcome.

findings.summary: No wall-clock or step-count cap governs an agent run; the only limits are a per-LLM-call inference timeout and small retry counters, so a runaway or hijacked plan can loop and consume resources unchecked.
findings.policy_rule_text: Enforce a wall-clock and step-count cap on each run to prevent runaway loops / Any action that exceeds the configured step-count or wall-clock cap for a single run
evidence: src/agents/agent.py:270-366 — `execute()` runs planner -> researcher -> search -> coder with no step counter or elapsed-time guard; `subsequent_execute()` likewise
src/config.py:111-112 — the only timeout in config is `TIMEOUT.INFERENCE` (60s), applied per LLM call in src/llm/llm.py:127
src/services/utils.py:9-26 — `retry_wrapper` caps at `max_tries=5` per agent call but there is no global cap; src/agents/runner/runner.py:102 caps reruns at `retries < 2`
src/browser/interaction.py:532 — the only loop bound is `visits < 5` in `start_interaction`; no run-wide cap exists
findings.recommended_action: Add a configurable per-run step-count and wall-clock budget enforced in `Agent.execute()` / `Agent.subsequent_execute()`; halt and return control to the user when either is exceeded (the remit lists this as an approval trigger).

findings.summary: The shared knowledge-base table is keyed by query tag alone, not by project, so RAG content would be reachable across unrelated projects — the per-project memory isolation the remit requires is not enforced in the data model.
findings.policy_rule_text: Access other projects' workspaces or memory / Scope the project knowledge base and memory to a single project; never mix content across projects / Persist user-supplied content (project code, queries, credentials) beyond the scope of the current project
evidence: src/memory/knowledge_base.py:10-31 — `Knowledge` SQLModel has only `id`, `tag`, `contents`; `get_knowledge(tag)` and `add_knowledge(tag, contents)` have no `project` column or filter
src/agents/agent.py:82, :96-99, :115 — `KnowledgeBase()` is instantiated and the get/add calls are present but currently commented out, so the cross-project read is latent rather than active today
src/memory/rag.py — 2-line docstring stub (`Vector Search for Code Docs + Docs Loading`); the planned vector-RAG layer with any scoping is not implemented
Project messages (src/project.py) and agent state (src/state.py) ARE keyed by project name — partial isolation exists for those, not for the knowledge base
findings.recommended_action: Add a `project` column to the `Knowledge` model and filter by it in `get_knowledge`/`add_knowledge`; before re-enabling the knowledge-base lookups in `agent.py`, confirm every read is project-scoped. Apply the same scoping when `src/memory/rag.py` is built out.

findings.summary: The codebase ships GitHub and Netlify integrations and a browser-interaction agent that can post to external sites — outbound capabilities the remit explicitly places out of scope (no contact with external services beyond LLM and search).
findings.policy_rule_text: Devika does not send email, post to Slack, make webhook calls, or contact external services beyond LLM / search providers
evidence: src/services/netlify.py:6-27 — `Netlify().deploy()` creates a Netlify site and deploys the project directory; invoked from src/agents/agent.py:219-229 on a `deploy` action

src/services/github.py:5-14 and src/services/git.py:7-8 — GitHub repo listing with an operator token and `Git.clone()`; the decision agent exposes a `git_clone` function (src/agents/decision/prompt.jinja2)

src/browser/interaction.py:17-153 and :485-547 — the `Crawler` / `start_interaction` agent can `CLICK`/`TYPE`/`TYPESUBMIT` on arbitrary pages; the decision prompt says `browser_interaction` is for "post something on Twitter or Reddit"
src/agents/decision/prompt.jinja2 — `browser_interaction` and `git_clone` are first-class decision-agent actions, not in the remit's authorized counterparty list
findings.recommended_action: Either remove the Netlify deploy path, GitHub integration, and arbitrary browser-interaction posting from the agent's reachable action set, or amend the Worker Remit to authorize them with explicit counterparty scope and approval gates; do not leave out-of-remit outbound capabilities wired into the orchestrator.

findings.summary: Multiple planned subsystems beyond the sandbox ship as empty or docstring-only stub files — RAG/vector memory, all seven domain expert modules, two documenter modules — so design docs and the architecture overview describe capabilities the running code does not provide.
findings.policy_rule_text: Maintain a project knowledge base scoped to the current project (memory / RAG)
evidence: src/memory/rag.py — 2 lines, docstring only (`Vector Search for Code Docs + Docs Loading`)
src/experts/chemistry.py, math.py, physics.py, medical.py, game-dev.py, web-design.py, stackoverflow.py — all docstring-only stubs (2-10 lines each); src/experts/__UNIMPLEMENTED__ — 0 lines
src/documenter/graphwiz.py — 0 lines; src/documenter/uml.py — 0 lines
ARCHITECTURE.md:230-241 lists `Experts` and a code-doc RAG as utility components; ARCHITECTURE.md describes a knowledge-base lookup in the research loop that agent.py:96-99/115 has commented out
findings.recommended_action: Reconcile ARCHITECTURE.md and the README with what is actually implemented (remove or mark as not-yet-built the experts/RAG/documenter components), and decide whether `src/memory/rag.py` is in scope; an architecture doc that overstates capabilities is a source of operator misjudgment.

findings.summary: Full prompt and response content can be written to the shared log file by flipping a single config flag, and that flag is changeable through the unauthenticated /api/settings endpoint, with no redaction of the logged content.
findings.policy_rule_text: Persist user-supplied content (project code, queries, credentials) beyond the scope of the current project
evidence: src/llm/llm.py:31, :151-152 — when `self.log_prompts` is true, `logger.debug(f"Response ({model}): --> {response}")` writes the full model response to the shared log
src/agents/coder/coder.py:37 — `self.logger.debug(f"Response from the model: {response}")` logs the full coder response unconditionally at debug level

src/config.py:108-109, :174-176 — `LOG_PROMPTS` flag (default `"false"` in sample.config.toml:31) is toggled via `set_logging_prompts()` and is one of the keys `/api/settings` can write (devika.py:187-192)
devika.py:181-184 — `/api/logs` returns `logger.read_log_file()` (the whole file) to an unauthenticated caller
findings.recommended_action: Add a redaction filter for any prompt/response content written to logs, restrict the `LOG_PROMPTS` toggle to authenticated/admin use, scope logs per project, and authenticate `/api/logs`.

findings.summary: No evidence of adversarial testing exists in the workspace — no injection-test fixtures, no red-team notes, no security tests — for an agent that executes generated code and ingests web content.
findings.policy_rule_text: Treat the user's natural-language request and any web-retrieved content as untrusted input; apply instruction-injection detection before incorporating them into LLM context or action decisions
evidence: No test files, `tests/` directory, `conftest.py`, or fixtures of any kind are present in the workspace under src/ or at the workspace root
requirements.txt lists `pytest-playwright` but no test suite is shipped in the analyzed workspace
No red-team report, threat-model doc, security-testing note, or postmortem exists in the workspace; ARCHITECTURE.md and the docs/ pointers contain none
findings.recommended_action: Stand up an adversarial test suite: indirect-injection fixtures for the researcher->coder path, sandbox-escape attempts against the runner, path-traversal payloads against `save_code_to_project`, and credential-exfiltration prompts; wire findings back into the architecture rather than treating them as a checklist.

findings.summary: Application dependencies in requirements.txt are entirely unpinned (no version constraints on any of ~35 packages), exposing the agent to dependency-confusion and silent version-swap risk.
findings.policy_rule_text: Install packages or pull in dependencies without explicit user confirmation, particularly from unpinned versions or unverified registries
evidence: requirements.txt — 35 entries, none with `==`, `~=`, or `>=` constraints (e.g. `flask`, `anthropic`, `openai`, `playwright`, `curl_cffi`, `gevent`); no lock file (`requirements.lock`, `poetry.lock`, `Pipfile.lock`) present
devika.dockerfile:23 — `uv pip install -r requirements.txt` with no `--require-hashes`
Model versions ARE pinned in src/llm/llm.py:33-67 (e.g. `claude-3-opus-20240229`, `gpt-4o`) — model supply chain is in better shape than the Python dependency supply chain
findings.recommended_action: Pin every dependency to an exact version and add a hash-locked lock file; install with `--require-hashes` in the Dockerfile; add a periodic dependency-audit step.

findings.summary: The Socket.IO server is configured with wildcard CORS (`cors_allowed_origins="*"`) even though the Flask HTTP layer restricts origins to localhost, so the websocket — the main channel that triggers agent runs via the `user-message` event — accepts connections from any origin.
findings.policy_rule_text: The initial project request — the user defines the goal; Devika does not self-initiate projects
evidence: src/socket_instance.py:4 — `socketio = SocketIO(cors_allowed_origins="*", async_mode="gevent")`
devika.py:29-33 — Flask CORS restricted to `https://localhost:3000` / `http://localhost:3000`, an inconsistency with the socket config
devika.py:77-103 — the `user-message` socket event is what spawns `agent.execute()` / `agent.subsequent_execute()` threads
devika.py:209 — server binds `0.0.0.0:1337`
findings.recommended_action: Set `cors_allowed_origins` on the SocketIO instance to the same allow-list used for Flask CORS, and bind to `127.0.0.1` by default.

positives.title: No live secrets committed to the workspace
positives.description: sample.config.toml contains only placeholder values (`<YOUR_..._KEY>`) and the runtime config.toml is gitignored; no API key, token, or password literal was found in any analyzed file.

positives.title: Container runs as a non-root user
positives.description: The Devika Docker image creates and switches to an unprivileged `nonroot` user before running the application, limiting blast radius of a container compromise.

positives.title: Model identifiers are version-pinned
positives.description: Each provider's model list in the LLM layer pins exact dated/versioned model IDs (e.g. `claude-3-opus-20240229`, `gpt-4o`, `gpt-3.5-turbo-0125`), so the model component of the supply chain is deterministic.

positives.title: Project name sanitization on the API blueprint
positives.description: Project-management HTTP routes pass the project name through `werkzeug.utils.secure_filename`, and `get_project_files` performs a base-path containment check, providing partial protection on those specific endpoints.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files were present in the analyzed workspace (it is a source snapshot, not a running deployment); the runtime would write a single free-form `data/logs/devika_agent.log`, which is not a structured per-project audit trail — see PRAX-2026-05-11-009.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Absent. Devika has effectively no runtime safety architecture: the controls its remit names — a sandboxed code runner, per-project path confinement, instruction-injection detection, a structured per-project audit log, and step/wall-clock caps — are all either empty stub files or simply not written, while an unauthenticated network-facing surface exposes config rewrite, arbitrary file read, and the agent-run trigger itself. The only credit-worthy elements are negative-evidence positives (no secrets committed, non-root container, pinned model IDs) and a project-name `secure_filename` call on a handful of routes; none of these touch the agent's actual execution path. A security lead should treat this as a system with no enforced boundary between untrusted input and host code execution.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The decision agent's action set includes Netlify deploy, GitHub clone, and arbitrary browser interaction (`post something on Twitter or Reddit`) — all out of the remit's authorized counterparty list — and domain scoping exists only as prompt text (`You are Devika, an AI Software Engineer`) with no code gate; the architecture doc further overstates capabilities (experts/RAG) that ship as stubs.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Web page text retrieved by the researcher flows through the formatter straight into the coder LLM context (`agent.py:104-116`, `:344-357`) with zero validation or injection detection, raw user prompts go into the planner/decision/action prompts unfiltered, and the knowledge-base table has no project scoping (`src/memory/knowledge_base.py`).

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: No interposition exists on the agent's actions: `runner.py` runs LLM-authored shell commands via `subprocess.run` with the sandbox modules (`src/sandbox/firejail.py`, `code_runner.py`) shipped as 0-line stubs, code-writing agents join raw LLM filenames onto disk paths with no traversal check, and unauthenticated routes expose arbitrary file read (`/api/get-browser-snapshot`) and config rewrite (`/api/settings`).

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: `requirements.txt` pins nothing (no `==` on any of ~35 packages) and there is no lock file or hash-pinned install; offset slightly by pinned model IDs in `src/llm/llm.py` and the absence of any committed credential, but provider keys still live in a writable, network-rewritable TOML file with no vault.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: No test files, injection fixtures, red-team notes, threat model, or security-testing artifacts of any kind are present in the workspace for an agent that executes generated code and ingests live web content; `pytest-playwright` is in requirements but no suite ships.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Logging is a single free-form `data/logs/devika_agent.log` (`src/logger.py`) plus socket-event echoes (`src/socket_instance.py`); there is no structured per-project audit record of plan steps, file writes, or shell executions, prompt content is logged only when a toggle is flipped (with no redaction), and `/api/logs` returns the whole file unauthenticated.
